---
title: "COMP 1150 — Computer Science Concepts"
subtitle: "Summer 2026"
author: "Brendan Shea, PhD"
date: last-modified
---

A twelve-week introduction to computer science. Each week pairs a
hands-on Jupyter notebook with a short case study on the people,
arguments, and history behind the technical material. The notebooks
teach you to write code with the help of an AI pair-programmer; the
case studies ask you to think about what that code does in the world.

## Notebooks

Each notebook is available three ways: **Read** (accessible HTML in the
browser), **Run** (open in Google Colab to execute and edit), and
**Download** (the raw `.ipynb`).

| # | Notebook | Read | Run | Download |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | What Is Computing? | [HTML](notebooks/COMP1150_NB01_WhatIsComputing.html) | [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/brendanpshea/computing_concepts_python/blob/main/summer_2026/notebooks/COMP1150_NB01_WhatIsComputing.ipynb) | [.ipynb](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/brendanpshea/computing_concepts_python/main/summer_2026/notebooks/COMP1150_NB01_WhatIsComputing.ipynb) |
| 2 | Machine Architecture & Data Representation | [HTML](notebooks/COMP1150_NB02_MachineArchitecture.html) | [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/brendanpshea/computing_concepts_python/blob/main/summer_2026/notebooks/COMP1150_NB02_MachineArchitecture.ipynb) | [.ipynb](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/brendanpshea/computing_concepts_python/main/summer_2026/notebooks/COMP1150_NB02_MachineArchitecture.ipynb) |
| 3 | Python via Pseudocode & Flowcharts | [HTML](notebooks/COMP1150_NB03_PseudocodeFlowcharts.html) | [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/brendanpshea/computing_concepts_python/blob/main/summer_2026/notebooks/COMP1150_NB03_PseudocodeFlowcharts.ipynb) | [.ipynb](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/brendanpshea/computing_concepts_python/main/summer_2026/notebooks/COMP1150_NB03_PseudocodeFlowcharts.ipynb) |
| 4 | Control Flow & Functions | [HTML](notebooks/COMP1150_NB04_ControlFlowFunctions.html) | [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/brendanpshea/computing_concepts_python/blob/main/summer_2026/notebooks/COMP1150_NB04_ControlFlowFunctions.ipynb) | [.ipynb](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/brendanpshea/computing_concepts_python/main/summer_2026/notebooks/COMP1150_NB04_ControlFlowFunctions.ipynb) |
| 5 | Collections & ADTs | [HTML](notebooks/COMP1150_NB05_CollectionsADTs.html) | [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/brendanpshea/computing_concepts_python/blob/main/summer_2026/notebooks/COMP1150_NB05_CollectionsADTs.ipynb) | [.ipynb](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/brendanpshea/computing_concepts_python/main/summer_2026/notebooks/COMP1150_NB05_CollectionsADTs.ipynb) |
| 6 | Functions, Modules & OOP | *in progress* |
| 7 | Algorithms & the Limits of Computation | *in progress* |
| 8 | Software Engineering, Git & AI-Assisted Dev | *in progress* |
| 9 | Databases: Relational & Non-Relational | *in progress* |
| 10 | OS, Networks, Cloud & the Web | *in progress* |
| 11 | Cybersecurity & Secure Software Development | *in progress* |
| 12 | AI, Machine Learning & Ethics | *in progress* |

## Case studies

Each case study takes about twenty to thirty minutes to read carefully.
There is no answer key — the discussion questions are for thinking and
arguing about with other people, not for looking up.

See the [case studies index](cases/index.qmd) for the full list. The
four drafted so far:

- [Lovelace and Turing: Can a Machine Originate?](cases/cs01_lovelace_turing.qmd)
- [The Silicon Shield: TSMC, Moore's Law, and the New Chip Wars](cases/cs02_silicon_shield.qmd)
- [Grace Hopper, Compilers & the Abstraction Bargain](cases/cs03_hopper_compilers.qmd)
- [The Therac-25: When Control Flow Kills](cases/cs04_therac25.qmd)

## How the pieces fit together

- **Read the notebook first.** Run the code. Do the exercises.
- **Then read the paired case study.** It tells the human story behind
  what you just learned and opens a debate worth your own opinion on.
- **Bring both to class.** The discussion questions are written to be
  argued out loud.

## License & source

Content licensed under
[Creative Commons Attribution 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).
Source on
[GitHub](https://github.com/brendanpshea/computing_concepts_python).
